step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

Therapist: Okay, that's a great starting point. I want to explain the 5As of tobacco cessation counseling, which are Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, and Arrange. Today, I'd like to advise you on the health risks associated with smoking and the benefits of quitting. Did you know that smoking increases your risk of heart disease, lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease ? Quitting can greatly reduce these risks and improve your overall health.
Patient: Yeah, I've heard that before, but I didn't think it applied to me.

Therapist: I understand that you feel like you're an exception to the rule, Jake. But the stats are clear: smoking is a major risk factor for many serious health problems. Let's explore how quitting might align with your values and goals. What's important to you in your life right now?
Patient: Well, I want to be around for my kids, and I want to be able to play sports with them without getting winded.

Therapist: That's great, Jake. It sounds like your family is important to you, and you want to be healthy for them. Quitting smoking can help you achieve that goal. Have you thought about how smoking might be affecting your daily life, beyond just your health? For example, how does it affect your relationships or your work?

Patient: Honestly, no. I don't really think about it that way. I mean, my friends and family are all cool with it, and it's just something I do. And at work, it's just a break thing. But I guess I do spend a lot of money on cigarettes... and sometimes I wish I didn't have to take so many breaks to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: A relapse does not erase the progress made. The most useful question after a slip is not why did I fail but what was happening just before â€” what was the trigger, what was the situation. That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.
Withdrawal symptoms from nicotine may include irritability, anxiety, difficulty concentrating, increased appetite, and strong cravings.
Sleep problems may occur after quitting smoking, but these usually improve after a few weeks.
